title: The Transfer Learning Paradigm
concept: Instead of training a model from scratch, pretrain it on a massive unlabeled text corpus, then fine-tune it on a small labeled dataset.

exercises.prompt: What are the two main phases of phases make up the transfer learning paradigm?
exercises.explain: Pretraining helps the model learn general language patterns, while fine-tuning adapts it to a specific task.

cards.title: Don't Start From Scratch!
cards.body: Imagine if you had to relearn how to read and write from scratch every time you wanted to study a new book. That sounds exhausting, right? For a long time, computers did exactly that! But transfer learning changed everything.

cards.title: What is Transfer Learning?
cards.body: Transfer learning is a technique where a model is first pretrained on a giant set of general text to learn the basics of language, and is then fine-tuned on a smaller, specific dataset to master a target task.

cards.title: The Musical Shortcut
cards.body: Imagine you want to learn to play jazz piano. Instead of starting from scratch without knowing what a piano keys look like, you already know how to play classical piano. You simply adapt your existing hand coordination, music theory, and finger strength to the new style of jazz.

cards.title: Language to Medicine
cards.body: A model is pretrained on millions of Wikipedia articles and web pages to understand general grammar. Then, developers fine-tune it on just 1,000 medical reports so it can help identify doctor diagnoses.

cards.title: The Scratch Myth
cards.body: Many believe that you must train an AI model from scratch on your specific dataset to get the best results. In reality, models that start with pretraining on general text almost always perform better and require much less of your own data!

cards.title: Visualizing the Paradigm
cards.body: Instead of training from scratch for every single task, we reuse the foundation built during pretraining.

left.text: Build a new foundation and house for every single task. Slow and expensive.

right.text: Build one solid foundation (pretraining) and just swap the roof (fine-tuning) for different tasks.

exercises.prompt: Why do we pretrain a model on a massive text corpus first?

options.text: To learn general grammar and language patterns.
To memorize the exact answers to a specific test.
To make the model run slower on mobile devices.
To delete any prior knowledge it had.

exercises.explain: Pretraining gives the model a broad, general understanding of how words, grammar, and sentences work before it tries to solve a specific problem.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Transfer learning requires you to collect millions of labeled examples for your specific task to work.
exercises.explain: Transfer learning actually requires much less labeled data because the model already understands basic language structure from the pretraining phase.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step in the transfer learning process.
exercises.sentence: First we pretrain the model on general text, and then we _____ it on a small labeled dataset.
exercises.explain: We pretrain the model to give it general language skills, and then fine-tune it to teach it the specific task we want it to solve.

exercises.prompt: Which AI is utilizing the Transfer Learning paradigm?

options.text: An AI that starts from scratch and learns English by reading only 50 customer reviews.
An AI that reads millions of web pages first, then practices on 50 customer reviews to learn sentiment analysis.
An AI that memorizes a dictionary and does not look at any customer reviews.

exercises.explain: A model that uses transfer learning will build upon its pretrained knowledge of English, rather than starting from absolute zero like the first model.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes how the Transfer Learning paradigm works?

options.text: It trains a model from scratch using only a tiny dataset.
It transfers files from one computer folder to another.
It takes general knowledge from a pretraining phase and refines it for a specific task.
It deletes old knowledge to make room for brand new rules.

exercises.explain: Transfer learning starts with general knowledge gained from pretraining on a massive dataset, and then fine-tunes that knowledge on a specific, smaller task. This saves massive amounts of time and resources!
